t: Does your child try to get your attention? (e.g., looking at you, for praise, saying 'look!')
Does your child respond to understood words? (e.g., 'give me the ball' or 'take the book')
If something new or strange happens, does your child look at your face to see your reaction?
Does your child enjoy active games? (For example, bouncing in air, hopping on knees)
Risk score = number of YES responses
exception: for items 2, 5, and 12 answer NO — risk indicator (YES answer on these three items considered risk).
Additional assessment is not required (if no other risk factors); rescreening recommended up to 24 months.
– structured follow-up interview; conducted by medical staff
Urgent ASP diagnostic assessment and referral to an early intervention program is required

M-CHAT-R – used in various clinics in Azerbaijani, but official validation in the Azerbaijani population is limited. The above translation is for clinical practice; original official translation should be used in scientific research (for official translations: https://mchatscreen.com).
B.2. AQ-10: Short Autism Spectrum Quotient (16 years+)
Carrie Allison, Bonnie Auyeung, Simon Baron-Cohen, 2012.
Allison C, Auyeung B, Baron-Cohen S. Toward brief "red flags" for autism screening: the short Autism Spectrum Quotient (AQ-10).
J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry
What do you think about the following 10 statements? For each statement
Strongly agree, Somewhat agree, Somewhat disagree, Strongly disagree
I often notice small sounds that others do not notice
I usually pay more attention to the overall picture rather than small details
I have difficulty following a conversation among several people in a social situation
I often focus so much on one topic or activity that I lose track of other things
I easily understand what someone is thinking or feeling when they say something
I am good at reading people's intentions
I have difficulty understanding what characters are thinking when reading a story
I like to collect detailed information about activities I have done before (e.g., car models, birds, trains)
I have difficulty understanding what a person is thinking or feeling when I look at them
I have difficulty knowing how to behave in social situations
"Absolutely not agree / Partially not agree" – 1 point
ASD diagnostic assessment probability is low
Detailed ASP clinical assessment is recommended
AQ-10 is a short version of the classic AQ-50. It is officially recommended by NICE CG142 (UK) for adult ASD screening in primary care. Sensitivity ~77%, specificity 36–41%.
AQ-10 is particularly reliable in "self screening" and should be used together with clinician's initial interview.
B.3. ASRS v1.1: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale
WHO, Ronald C. Kessler and colleagues, Harvard Medical School, 2005.
Kessler RC, Adler L, Ames M, et al. The World Health Organization Adult DDHP Self-Report Scale (ASRS).
This scale is for screening HDHD symptoms in adults. The following 6 questions –
Answer based on how you felt. For each question
Never, Rarely, Sometimes, Often, Very often
How often do you have difficulty finishing the final details after a difficult part of a project is completed?
How often do you have difficulty prioritizing tasks when organizing a demanding job?
How often do you have trouble remembering appointments or obligations?
When you have a task that requires a lot of thinking, how often do you hesitate to start or procrastinate?
How often do you move or fidget your hands and feet when you have to sit for a long time?
How often do you feel 'motor-driven' hyperactive or compelled to do something?
Responses in the bold cells of the table — a positive sign.
Symptoms not typical for adult ADHD
Symptoms highly consistent with adult ADHD. Medical evaluation recommended.
Part B – Extended questionnaire (12 additional questions)

When Part A is positive, Part B questions (12 additional questions) are used in conjunction with clinical assessment. The full version of Part B can be obtained from the developers' official website: https://www.hcp.med.harvard.edu/ncs/asrs.php
ASRS v1.1 – Developed by WHO, the most widely used adult ADHD screening scale in the world. By Kessler et al. worldwide
Processed — a shorter 5-question version. Both versions are suitable for screening; ASRS v1.1 remains widely used.
B.4. SNAP-IV: Swanson, Nolan, and Pelham Rating Scale (child ADHD)
Swanson JM. School-based assessments and interventions for ADD students. Irvine, CA: KC Publishing, 1992.
SNAP-IV – the assessment of ADHD symptoms in children based on DSM-IV/APA, 1994 criteria, DSM-IV/APA, 2013 – DSM-5 criteria.
To be completed by parent or teacher.
How is each symptom observed in your child over the past 1 month:
Often makes careless mistakes in schoolwork or other activities.
Often has difficulty sustaining attention in tasks or play.
Often appears not to listen when spoken to directly.
Often fails to follow instructions and to finish schoolwork or daily chores.
Often has difficulty organizing tasks and activities.
Often avoids, dislikes, or reluctantly performs tasks requiring sustained mental effort.
Often loses necessary items for tasks or activities (school materials, pencils, books).
Often easily distracted by extraneous stimuli.
Often fidgets with hands or feet or squirms in seat.
Often leaves seat in room when remaining seated is required.
Often runs about or climbs excessively in inappropriate situations.
